narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 60.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your family's high income bracket and plans to attend full-time. The main reasons for this outcome centered on your class rankings from earlier and later years in school, which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting a bar exam struggle. Specifically, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades had the biggest influence, signaling to the model a notable dip compared to the typical fifth or sixth decile among students who passed the bar. Your Year 1 ranking in the third decile reinforced this concern, adding further weight toward the failure prediction, especially since passers averaged around the sixth decile. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success because it's stronger than the 3.24 average for those who passed. Your LSAT score of 35, while below the 37.27 average for passers, played a lesser role here, as did your high family income, which is less common among successful bar takers but didn't sway things much either. Compared to admitted students, who often have higher early rankings and bar passage rates around 90% for full-timers like you, your profile showed these ranking gaps as the key mismatches. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar starts improve dramatically—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students to minimize risks for our program. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and focus on strategies to climb into higher deciles in any upcoming academic work, perhaps through targeted tutoring or advanced courses. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience could also help reframe your story in a future application. With your solid undergrad performance as a foundation, targeted improvements in these areas could shift the odds convincingly in your favor next time.
